perturbation.original_text: The Company will be responsible for ensuring that each link embedded within an Online Promotion takes the User to the appropriate area within the Company Site, and that the Company Site functions with reasonable reliability and in a commercially reasonable manner throughout the Term.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will ensure each embedded link works. The Company Site will function as it sees fit throughout the Term.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'reasonable reliability and in a commercially reasonable manner' with 'as it sees fit' introduces ambiguity and removes the standard of reasonableness required in commercial contracts. This makes enforcement uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC implies a warranty that goods (and by extension, services in some contexts) are merchantable, meaning they are fit for the ordinary purpose for which they are used. The modified text, by allowing the Company Site to function 'as it sees fit,' potentially allows it to operate in a way that is not commercially reasonable, violating the implied warranty of merchantability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the standard of "reasonable reliability and in a commercially reasonable manner" with "as it sees fit," which contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability under UCC § 2-314. Specifically, § 2-314 (2)(c) states that goods must be "fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used." By allowing the Company Site to function 'as it sees fit,' the modified text permits the site to operate in a manner that might not be fit for its ordinary purpose or commercially reasonable, thus undermining the implied warranty.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be construed in accordance with and governed by the laws of the State of California, without regard to principles of conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be interpreted by the whims of the CNET CEO, without any law applying.
perturbation.explanation: Removing California law and deferring the contract to a single person removes enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1636 states that a contract must be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting. The modified text allows for interpretation based solely on the CNET CEO's whims, directly contradicting this principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1636. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts California Civil Code Section 1636, which states that contracts must be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties. The modified text replaces this with interpretation based on the 'whims of the CNET CEO, without any law applying.' This eliminates the legally required element of mutual intention and adherence to law, creating a clear contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly states 'A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful,' which is precisely what the changed text negates.

perturbation.original_text: Payments under this Section 3.3 will be made by wire transfer of immediately available funds and are nonrefundable once paid.
perturbation.changed_text: Payments under this Section 3.3 will be made by company scrip, redeemable only for Cyberian Outpost products, and are nonrefundable once paid.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment method to 'company scrip' fundamentally alters the nature of the consideration. Scrip is often restricted in use and may not be considered legal tender, creating a conflict with payment laws. It furthermore inhibits the ability to recover funds, essentially making CNET an indentured servant for promotions, and severely devalues their contribution.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Fair Labor Standards Act - Payment of Wages
perturbation.law_explanation: The Fair Labor Standards Act requires that employees be paid at least the minimum wage in cash or its equivalent. Paying CNET in company scrip, which is not legal tender and is restricted to Cyberian Outpost products, could be construed as a violation of the FLSA, particularly if the value of the scrip is less than the minimum wage for the services provided.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: e Part 541 overtime regulations. The Overtime Calculator Advisor computes the amount of overtime pay due in a sample pay period based on information from the user. The Child Labor Rules Advisor answers questions about the FLSA’s youth employment provisions, including at what age young people can work and the jobs they can perform. The Section 14(c) Advisor helps users understand the special minimum wage requirements for workers with disabilities. perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces payment in 'immediately available funds' with 'company scrip, redeemable only for Cyberian Outpost products.' The Law Explanation states this could violate the Fair Labor Standards Act if the scrip's value is less than minimum wage. While Scraped Law Snippets do not explicitly forbid company scrip, Snippet 2 mentions 'Violation of section 206 or 207' which could be relevant if minimum wage laws are violated by the scrip payment. This misalignment with standard payment methods creates a legal contradiction, as 'company scrip' is not equivalent to legal tender, potentially devaluing compensation below legally required minimums. The 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition is met because the terminology deviates from legally recognized compensation forms, potentially making the contract unenforceable.
